what_it_does: This file is a prescriptive constitution for the voice/ears module. It defines the speech-IN twin of voice/engines as a set of isolated HTTP STT services (one per ear), a shared inference contract, a port map, lazy-import and fail-loud guarantees, registry-driven selection, onboarding rules for new ears, capability writeups for compact realtime ONNX ears, and explicit anti-patterns.
observations: voice/ears is described as the speech-IN twin of voice/engines, with one HTTP STT service per ear mirroring the contract in voice/ears/_stt_service.py.
The shared contract is POST /inference with multipart file=@clip.wav returning {"text":...}, plus GET / and /health returning {"ok":true,"ear":<name>}.
Port map: whispercpp:2022 (external), parakeet:2031, canary:2032, granite:2033, moonshine:2034, parakeet-onnx:2035.
Heavy imports (NeMo/transformers) must live inside a lazy _engine(); missing libraries fail loud with a pip install message only at model load time.
warm() must fail loud at startup; available_stt() probes the port and reports available:false plus detail; there is no silent fallback to whisper.cpp.
Registry-is-truth: each ear is a STT_PROVIDERS row in voice/stt.py; UI/RHM list from available_stt(); active ear is changed via rhm_config().stt, never code edits.
New ear onboarding: add voice/ears/<name>.py with transcribe and optional warm, call _stt_service.serve, add a registry row, an ops/services.json row, and a REQUIREMENTS.md entry.
Compact realtime ears (lane 5): moonshine on ONNX runtime and parakeet-onnx via sherpa-onnx int8, CPU-default, 0 VRAM, sub-1–1.5 GB, measured on an RTX 4080 / i7-12700KF as of 2026-06-28.
moonshine emphasizes lowest latency, short-clip speed, English focus, an IntentRecognizer, and VAD/tokens-per-second dials.
parakeet-onnx emphasizes ~6.3% WER, 25 European languages, hotword/context biasing, and a ~670 MB int8 model.
The seam is only through voice/stt.py transcribe(audio, provider=...); ears do not touch the Suite or store.
A Never section forbids top-level heavy imports, shared venvs, fabricated transcripts, unchecked GPU loading, and silent fallback.
An honest boundary note distinguishes lane 5 ears as verified by use from the heavy GPU ears (:2031–:2033) which remain run-once/estimated.
standouts: The one-contract-per-port, registry-is-truth architecture with strict no-silent-fallback rule.
Guarded heavy imports plus fail-loud warm() as a reliability contract.
Lane 5 compact realtime ears (Moonshine, Parakeet-ONNX) as a CPU-default, 0-VRAM interaction loadout, with measured performance claims.
The explicit Never anti-patterns section and the honest verified-vs-estimated boundary note.
conventions: Follows the constitution format: type, register, module, aliases, tags, governs, relates-to, status: living.
Uses port-per-service and per-ear venv isolation.
Uses lazy imports and loud, precise failure at warm().
Uses registry-driven configuration rather than hardcoded routing.
Capability writeups follow a consistent structure: what, qualities, measured, tuning dials, use.
Includes an explicit boundary/honesty note distinguishing verified and estimated claims.
concerns: Some dates (e.g., 2026-06-28) appear future-dated, which may confuse readers about when measurements were taken.
The heavy GPU ears (:2031–:2033) are explicitly noted as run-once/estimated, leaving a capability/verification gap relative to lane 5.
The document mixes prescriptive rules, operational runbook, and performance measurements in one file.
Never rules depend on external enforcement (registry probes, venv tooling) that is not shown in this file.
Port numbers and environment variables are listed here while registry-is-truth is asserted, creating a potential duplication/maintenance risk.
notes: A prescriptive constitution that turns each STT backend into an isolated HTTP ear behind a registry, with strict rules about lazy imports, loud failures, and no silent fallback.
questions: How is available_stt() implemented and how frequently does it probe each port?
What is the exact schema of STT_PROVIDERS in voice/stt.py?
How does company up create and activate the per-ear venvs listed in REQUIREMENTS.md?
What is the interaction combo class and how does it choose between moonshine and parakeet-onnx?
How are CUDA-13 hazards detected and reported during warm()?
What is the Speech-To-Action (STA) command core and how are intents registered?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose and content align: it is a prescriptive constitution for the ears organ, covering contracts, guarantees, anti-patterns, and onboarding.
apparent_themes: Isolation through ports, venvs, and lazy imports
Measured honesty and explicit verification status
intention_signals: It appears designed to keep the speech-IN subsystem from becoming a fragile monolith by enforcing one-service-per-ear, guarded lazy imports, explicit registry-based routing, and honest reporting of which capabilities have actually been exercised.
novelty: The lane 5 compact realtime ears concept with explicit 0-VRAM CPU-default loadout, the hard Never anti-patterns section, and the honest boundary note distinguishing verified-by-use ears from estimated ones.
contribution: It contributes a stable contract definition and port map, a registry-is-truth rule for ear selection, a guarded-import/fail-loud-warm reliability pattern, a per-ear venv and onboarding checklist, and capability writeups with an explicit verified-vs-estimated boundary.
summary_for_embedding: voice/ears/AGENTS.md is a living prescriptive constitution that defines an HTTP STT-service-per-ear architecture, a shared inference contract, a port map, lazy-import and fail-loud guarantees, registry-driven selection, and capability/anti-pattern rules for heavy GPU and compact realtime ONNX ears.
intention_for_embedding: It was written to keep the speech-IN subsystem modular, observable, honest about verification status, and free of silent fallbacks or heavy top-level imports.
